items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-1, FIG 6-2-2, and FIG 6-2-3. Two successive departures from the same or adjacent airports will fly courses diverging by at least 45 degrees. Which minimum matches an immediate divergence after takeoff?
items.explanation: For diverging courses after departure, the timing/distance condition selects the minimum. FIG 6-2-1 covers immediate divergence after takeoff: 1 minute until courses diverge.

choices.text: 1 minute until courses diverge.
2 minutes until courses diverge.
3 miles until courses diverge.
5 minutes until vertical separation exists.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-1, FIG 6-2-2, and FIG 6-2-3. The aircraft will diverge later in the departure path, within 5 minutes after takeoff. Which separation minimum applies until divergence?
items.explanation: The controlling principle is when the courses begin diverging. FIG 6-2-2 represents the within-5-minutes case, so the required minimum is 2 minutes until courses diverge; the 1-minute option is only for immediate divergence.

choices.text: 1 minute until courses diverge.
2 minutes until courses diverge.
3 miles until courses diverge.
10 minutes until courses diverge.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-3. Successive departures will diverge within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff. Which minimum is the DME/ATD-based diverging-course option?
items.explanation: When the diverging-course condition is framed as within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff, FIG 6-2-3 uses 3 miles until courses diverge.

choices.text: 2 minutes until courses diverge.
3 miles until courses diverge.
5 miles until courses diverge.
30 miles until courses diverge.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-4. In terminal operations, aircraft depart in the same direction from different parallel runways whose centerlines are at least 3,500 feet apart and will diverge immediately after takeoff. What does the figure support?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-4 illustrates the terminal rule for simultaneous takeoffs from different parallel runways in the same direction when runway centerlines are at least 3,500 feet apart and courses diverge immediately after takeoff.

choices.text: Only one aircraft may depart until the first is 10 minutes away.
Simultaneous takeoffs may be authorized under those stated conditions.
The aircraft must be separated by 30 miles because both use DME.
The rule applies only after both aircraft are en route over the same fix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-5. For nonintersecting diverging runways, which runway geometry permits simultaneous takeoffs when the divergence angle is 15 to 29 degrees inclusive?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-5 supports simultaneous takeoffs from nonintersecting diverging runways when the runways diverge by 30 degrees or more, or when centerline-distance criteria are met. For 15 to 29 degrees inclusive, at least 2,000 feet between centerlines at and beyond the takeoff start points is required.

choices.text: Any centerline spacing is sufficient if both aircraft are jets.
At least 2,000 feet between runway centerlines at and beyond the points where takeoffs begin.
At least 500 feet between runway centerlines at the thresholds only.
At least 30 miles between aircraft using DME/ATD.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-5. For nonintersecting runways that diverge by less than 15 degrees, what additional geometry is needed before simultaneous takeoffs may be authorized under this figure?
items.explanation: For nonintersecting diverging runways with less than 15 degrees of divergence, FIG 6-2-5 requires at least 3,500 feet between centerlines at and beyond the points where takeoffs begin.

choices.text: At least 3,500 feet between runway centerlines at and beyond the takeoff start points.
Only 1 minute between departures.
The preceding aircraft must have started a procedure turn.
The runways must intersect before the departure end.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-6 and FIG 6-2-7. On intersecting runways, which condition separates the 30-degrees-or-more case from the 15-to-29-degrees case?
items.explanation: For intersecting runways, both cases require the preceding aircraft to have passed the runway intersection. FIG 6-2-7 adds that for 15 to 29 degrees inclusive, the preceding aircraft must also have commenced a turn.

choices.text: The 15-to-29-degrees case also requires the preceding aircraft to have commenced a turn.
The 30-degrees-or-more case requires 30 miles DME/ATD.
The 15-to-29-degrees case requires only simultaneous release with no aircraft movement.
The two cases use identical conditions with no added turn requirement.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-8 and FIG 6-2-9. Two departures will fly the same course, and the following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft. Which minimum applies when time, rather than DME/ATD distance, is used?
items.explanation: For same-course successive departures where the following aircraft climbs through the leading aircraft's assigned altitude, the time-based minimum is 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through that altitude.

choices.text: 1 minute until the following aircraft starts takeoff roll.
3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft.
5 minutes until both aircraft are above 10,000 feet.
10 minutes until the leading aircraft reports a fix.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-8 and FIG 6-2-9. Which distance-based alternative can replace the 3-minute same-course climb-through minimum when the required DME/ATD conditions are met?
items.explanation: Paragraph 6-2-2 and the related figures allow 5 miles between DME-equipped aircraft, RNAV aircraft using ATD, and certain mixed DME/ATD combinations when the DME aircraft condition is satisfied.

choices.text: 2 miles.
3 miles.
5 miles.
30 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-3-1. No appropriate fix is available. The departure direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course. What timing must be met relative to the arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: When the fix-based terminal provisions cannot be used and the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of final, FIG 6-3-1 supports departure 3 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.

choices.text: The departure takes off 3 minutes before the arriving aircraft is estimated at the airport.
The departure takes off after the arriving aircraft crosses the threshold.
The departure waits until the arriving aircraft starts a procedure turn.
The departure takes off only after 30 miles DME separation exists.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-3-2 and FIG 6-3-3. No appropriate fix is available, and the takeoff direction falls outside the at-least-45-degree condition from the reciprocal final approach course. What must be achieved before the 5-minute arrival timing point?
items.explanation: When the takeoff direction is other than the FIG 6-3-1 case, the departing aircraft must be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of final 5 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport or before it starts procedure turn.

choices.text: The departure must be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal final approach course.
The arrival must cancel IFR before the departure rolls.
The departure must maintain the same course as final approach.
Both aircraft must use the same DME NAVAID within 10 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-3-1, FIG 6-3-2, and FIG 6-3-3. What is the key decision point when choosing between the 3-minute and 5-minute no-fix departure/arrival minima?
items.explanation: The 3-minute case applies when the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of final. Otherwise, the 5-minute case requires the departure to be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees by the specified arrival timing point.

choices.text: Whether the aircraft are the same wake category.
Whether the airport has a published STAR.
Whether the departure direction itself meets the 45-degree relationship to reciprocal final, or must first become established on a 45-degree diverging course.
Whether both aircraft are above FL180.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-3. An en route aircraft follows a preceding en route aircraft that has reported over the same fix, and the leading aircraft is at least 44 knots faster. Which minimum family applies?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-3 is the crossing-course version of the 44-knots-or-more rule. When the leading aircraft maintains at least 44 knots more speed, use 5 miles for DME/ATD cases or 3 minutes between other aircraft when the stated condition is met.

choices.text: 5 miles DME/ATD or 3 minutes between other aircraft under the 44-knots-or-more rule.
30 miles because one aircraft is always assumed non-DME.
10 minutes before and after estimated passing because this is opposite-course separation.
1 minute until courses diverge because both aircraft departed the same airport.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-4, FIG 6-4-5, and FIG 6-4-6. When the leading aircraft is at least 22 knots faster than the following aircraft, which longitudinal minimum replaces the 44-knot case?
items.explanation: For the 22-knots-or-more family, the minima are 10 miles for DME/ATD cases or 5 minutes between other aircraft, assuming one of the listed same/converging/crossing-course conditions is met.

choices.text: 5 miles DME/ATD or 3 minutes between other aircraft.
10 miles DME/ATD or 5 minutes between other aircraft.
20 miles DME/ATD or 10 minutes between other aircraft.
30 miles whenever DME and non-DME aircraft are mixed.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-7 and FIG 6-4-8. Which altitude-crossing geometry allows the 10-mile DME/ATD minimum when one aircraft climbs or descends through another aircraft's altitude?
items.explanation: For the 10-mile DME/ATD altitude-crossing minimum, the descending aircraft must be leading or the climbing aircraft must be following.

choices.text: The descending aircraft is leading, or the climbing aircraft is following.
The descending aircraft is always following, regardless of distance.
Either aircraft may climb or descend through the other without regard to sequence.
The aircraft must be on opposite courses with no altitude change.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-9 and FIG 6-4-10. For non-DME/ATD timed separation when one aircraft climbs or descends through another's altitude, which added conditions matter?
items.explanation: The 5-minute timed altitude-crossing option requires the descending aircraft to be leading or the climbing aircraft following, no more than 4,000 feet separation when the altitude change starts, and the change to start within the stated 10-minute/fix-clearance timing window.

choices.text: Only the aircraft call signs matter; altitude and timing are irrelevant.
Both aircraft must be in radar contact and already vertically separated by 10,000 feet.
Sequence, no-more-than-4,000-feet initial vertical difference, and the stated 10-minute/fix-clearance timing condition.
Both aircraft must be departing from parallel runways at least 3,500 feet apart.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-11, FIG 6-4-12, FIG 6-4-13, FIG 6-4-14, FIG 6-4-15, and FIG 6-4-16. When the special speed or altitude-crossing conditions in subparagraphs a, b, or c cannot be met, which fallback longitudinal minimum applies?
items.explanation: The fallback family in paragraph 6-4-2d uses 20 miles for DME/ATD cases or 10 minutes between other aircraft when the preceding speed or altitude-crossing conditions cannot be met.

choices.text: 1 minute or 2 miles.
5 miles or 3 minutes.
10 miles or 5 minutes.
20 miles or 10 minutes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-17 and FIG 6-4-18. One aircraft is using DME/ATD and the other aircraft lacks DME/ATD. What additional conditions support the 30-mile minimum?
items.explanation: For the mixed DME/ATD and non-DME case, the DME/ATD aircraft must derive distance from the same NAVAID or waypoint over which the non-DME aircraft reported, and the non-DME aircraft must be within 15 minutes of the NAVAID.

choices.text: The DME/ATD aircraft uses the same reference point over which the non-DME aircraft reported, and the non-DME aircraft is within 15 minutes of the NAVAID.
The aircraft are both on any published arrival, regardless of reference point.
The non-DME aircraft must be more than 30 minutes past the NAVAID.
The aircraft must be less than 2,000 feet apart laterally at departure.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-19. For aircraft traveling opposite courses, when must different altitudes be assigned before and after the estimated passing time?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-19 supports the opposite-course rule: assign different altitudes consistent with approved vertical separation from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after the aircraft are estimated to pass.

choices.text: From 1 minute before until 1 minute after estimated passing.
From 5 miles before until 5 miles after estimated passing.
From 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after estimated passing.
Only after both aircraft report the same waypoint.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-20. Which report combination allows vertical separation to be discontinued for opposite-course aircraft?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-20 illustrates discontinuing vertical separation after both aircraft have reported passing NAVAIDs, DME fixes, or waypoints indicating they have passed each other.

choices.text: Only one aircraft reports established on final.
Both aircraft report passing points that indicate they have passed each other.
The following aircraft reports climbing through the leading aircraft's altitude.
The aircraft are 1 minute apart on diverging departures.
